prompt:
Chapter 1

1:1 And behold there was set before thee a charge and a commandment saying Write thou a passage concerning that season of the year which is most beloved unto thine heart

1:2 And the manner of thy writing shall be thus that thou shalt employ no mark of punctuation whatsoever neither the full stop nor the mark of separation between clauses

1:3 Thou shalt set down no apostrophe within thy words nor shalt thou place any mark of questioning at the end of thine inquiries nor any mark of exclamation after thy declarations

1:4 Yea verily I say unto thee that no mark of punctuation of any kind soever shall appear in thy writing neither those which have been named nor any other marks which serve a like purpose

1:5 For this is the law and the commandment concerning thy composition that the words shall flow one unto another without ceasing and without the dividing marks which scribes are wont to place among their words

1:6 Go thou therefore and write concerning thy favored season and let thy pen move freely but let no punctuation dwell therein
response:
Chapter 2

2:1 And lo I shall speak unto thee of that season which is most beloved unto mine heart even the season of autumn which some do call the fall

2:2 For when the great heat of summer doth begin to loosen its grip upon the land and the air doth turn itself unto a gentle coolness then doth my spirit rise up within me with exceeding gladness

2:3 Behold the trees of the field do put on their garments of glory and their leaves do turn from the green of summer unto gold and crimson and burnished copper such as no craftsman could fashion with his hands

2:4 And the morning doth come forth with a mist upon the ground that doth hang low over the meadows and the rivers like a veil of silver spread across the face of the earth

2:5 In that season the harvest is gathered in and the fruits of the vine and of the orchard are brought unto the storehouse even the apples and the pears and the great pumpkins that do sit upon the earth like golden vessels

2:6 The air doth carry upon itself the fragrance of fallen leaves and of wood smoke rising from the hearths of the people who do prepare their dwellings against the coming of winter

2:7 And in the evenings the sun doth set earlier upon the land and doth paint the sky with colors of such beauty that the heart is made to wonder at the works of creation

2:8 The geese do gather themselves together in great companies and do fly across the heavens in their appointed formations crying out one unto another as they journey toward the south

2:9 And there is a stillness that doth settle upon the world in autumn a holy quietness as though the earth itself doth pause to draw breath before the long slumber of winter

2:10 Yea I say unto thee that autumn is a season of remembrance and of thanksgiving when the soul doth look upon the abundance that has been given and is made humble

2:11 For in the falling of the leaves there is a teaching that all things do pass through their seasons and that there is beauty even in the letting go

2:12 Therefore autumn shall remain the season most cherished within mine heart from this day forth and forevermore
